title: WanderingBong
description: A slow-travel blog with 38 posts, 198 indexed URLs, and an AI-assisted publishing pipeline I built and run in production. Telegram capture → custom MCP server → Claude API agent → Supabase CMS → published post. It's also the live testbed for the content platform I'm productizing as Roami.
lede: Started as a blog. Became a production AI pipeline. Now it's the proof-of-concept for a product I'm building.
story:
WanderingBong started as a WordPress site with a Notion folder full of notes I kept meaning to turn into posts. The content existed. The gap was always between having the trip and having the post — too many steps, too much friction, things getting lost.

So I rebuilt the whole stack from scratch.

## The Platform

The current architecture is a monorepo called ContentPlanner: a headless CMS backend (Node.js + Express + Prisma + PostgreSQL via Supabase), a Next.js admin dashboard, a Next.js public blog renderer, a custom MCP server, and a trip journal agent — five services, all running on an Oracle VPS behind HAProxy, orchestrated by PM2.

The blog at wanderingbong.com runs on port 3002. The CMS at cms.wanderingbong.com runs on port 3001. The API on port 3000. The MCP server on port 3003. SSL termination, routing, and www-to-apex redirects all go through HAProxy. Three domains live on the same VPS: wanderingbong.com, roami.xyz, and lyaadh.com — all isolated by HAProxy ACLs.

Infrastructure detail that mattered: getting the default backend wrong during a Roami deploy briefly served WanderingBong content at the Lyaadh domain. The fix was explicit deny-unknown-host as the HAProxy default — a lesson in why you don't let anything fall through to a catch-all.

## The Capture Pipeline

When I'm traveling and want to capture something — a restaurant, a viewpoint, a moment worth writing about later — I send it to a Telegram bot. The bot routes it through a Railway instance to the CMS backend, where it lands as a raw journal entry attached to the current trip. No context switching, no opening a note app, no remembering to sync later.

The trip journal system has its own Prisma schema (TripJournal, TripJournalEntry, TripJournalLog models), a full Telegram webhook handler, and a cms-agent service running on port 8790 that handles the parse and scaffold logic.

## The AI Publishing Layer

The interesting part is what happens when I'm ready to turn journal entries into posts.

I built cms-mcp — a custom MCP server that gives Claude full context about my content: what's published, what destinations I've covered, what's sitting in the journal backlog, how posts are tagged and clustered, what affiliate links are active. It exposes tools for creating drafts, managing posts, handling landing page blocks, managing newsletter campaigns, and querying the content graph.

A Google ADK agent reads the raw journal entries and drafts posts in my voice, informed by two years of published writing. The voice calibration matters more than the prompting — the agent has read everything I've written before it writes anything new.

I review, edit lightly, and publish through the CMS dashboard.

The first real test: five Thailand posts drafted in an afternoon from three weeks of journal entries. They sounded like me. Not because I prompted carefully, but because the agent had context — every previous post, every destination I'd covered, how I'd written about similar places before.

## The Content

38 posts published. 198 indexed URLs (posts, galleries, videos, destination landing pages, tag pages). SEO traction in the Karnataka/Bangalore day-trip cluster — posts like Chikmagalur, Hoysala architecture, and the hill forts series rank for competitive searches.

29 affiliate links live across Booking.com and Klook. First real posts to monetize: Ao Nang Krabi and Koh Lanta, which are 3,800+ word guides that already recommend specific hotels and activities. Revenue is still close to zero — the links are placed, the traffic is growing.

The content strategy is deliberately slow: first-person, lived experience only, opinionated over descriptive. Thailand and Karnataka are the two clusters with real depth. Vietnam has five posts. Kolkata and Rajasthan are smaller clusters.

Underutilized asset: 10+ destinations with gallery and video content but no corresponding blog post — Ladakh, Kerala, Kodaikanal, Ooty, Jaisalmer. Photos already exist. Writing is the bottleneck.

## The Bigger Picture

WanderingBong is now Customer Zero for Roami — the product I'm building on top of this same stack.

The ContentPlanner monorepo now serves three products: WanderingBong (the blog), Roami (a travel journaling app for anyone, not just me), and Lyaadh (a separate lifestyle publication). WanderingBong proved the pipeline works. Roami is the productized version of that pipeline for other people.

Every post I publish through the AI pipeline is a live demo. The workflow, the speed, the voice fidelity — it's all measurable here before it gets sold to anyone else. When the agent produces a post that sounds like me, the product is working. When it sounds generic, that's a bug to fix before beta.

The MCP integration is what I didn't expect to matter as much as it does. Being able to ask Claude what I've written about a region, mid-conversation, without breaking context — that's the version of the system that made me want to productize it.
